subject: Physics
q: A particle moving in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> with uniform speed takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> to complete one revolution. If this particle is projected with the same speed at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> to the horizontal, the maximum height attained by it is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The angle of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is then given by :
question: A particle moving in a circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> with uniform speed takes time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> to complete one revolution. If this particle is projected with the same speed at an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> to the horizontal, the maximum height attained by it is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The angle of projection <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is then given by :
solution:
Time period of circular motion is given by, 

2πRv=T⇒v=2πRT

Now, maximum height H=v2sin2θ2g

⇒4R=4π2R2T2sin2θ2g

⇒sinθ=2gT2π2R

⇒θ=sin−12gT2π2R12

subject: Physics
q: Water flows in a horizontal pipe whose one end is closed with a valve. The reading of the pressure gauge attached to the pipe is $P_1$. The reading of the pressure gauge falls to $P_2$ when the valve is opened. The speed of water flowing in the pipe is proportional to
question: Water flows in a horizontal pipe whose one end is closed with a valve. The reading of the pressure gauge attached to the pipe is $P_1$. The reading of the pressure gauge falls to $P_2$ when the valve is opened. The speed of water flowing in the pipe is proportional to
solution: Using Bernoulli's theorem $\begin{aligned} & P_1-P_2=\frac{1}{2} \rho v^2 \\ & v \propto \sqrt{P_1-P_2} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Consider a rubber ball freely falling from a height $h=4.9 \mathrm{~m}$ onto a horizontal elastic plate. Assume that the duration of collision is negligible and the collision with the plate is totally elastic. Then the velocity as a function of time the height as function of time will be
question: Consider a rubber ball freely falling from a height $h=4.9 \mathrm{~m}$ onto a horizontal elastic plate. Assume that the duration of collision is negligible and the collision with the plate is totally elastic. Then the velocity as a function of time the height as function of time will be
solution: $\mathrm{h}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{gt}^2, \quad \mathrm{v}=-\mathrm{gt}$ and after the collision, $\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{gt}$. (parabolic) (straight line) Collision is perfectly elastic then ball reaches to same height again and again with same velocity.

q: In a transformer, number of turns in the primary coil are 140 and that in the secondary coil are 280. If current in primary coil is $4 \mathrm{~A}$, then that in the secondary coil is
question: In a transformer, number of turns in the primary coil are 140 and that in the secondary coil are 280. If current in primary coil is $4 \mathrm{~A}$, then that in the secondary coil is
solution: $I_1 N_1=I_2 N_2 \Rightarrow I_2=\frac{4 \times 140}{280}=2 \mathrm{~A}$

subject: Physics
q: A printed page is pressed by a glass of water. The refractive index of the glass and water is $1.5$ and $1.33$, respectively. If the thickness of the bottom of glass is $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ and depth of water is $5 \mathrm{~cm}$, how much the page will appear to be shifted if viewed from the top ?
question: A printed page is pressed by a glass of water. The refractive index of the glass and water is $1.5$ and $1.33$, respectively. If the thickness of the bottom of glass is $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ and depth of water is $5 \mathrm{~cm}$, how much the page will appear to be shifted if viewed from the top ?
solution:
Real depth $=5 \mathrm{~cm}+1 \mathrm{~cm}=6 \mathrm{~cm}$
Apparent depth $=\frac{\mathrm{d}_1}{\mu_1}+\frac{\mathrm{d}_2}{\mu_2}+\ldots$ $=\frac{5}{1.33}+\frac{1}{1.5}$ $\simeq 3.8+0.7 \simeq 4.5 \mathrm{~cm}$ $\therefore$ Shift $=6 \mathrm{~cm}-4.5 \mathrm{~cm} \cong 1.5 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: A second's pendulum has a time period of 1 second.

Statement II: It takes precisely one second to move between the two extreme positions. In the light of the above statements,

choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I: A second's pendulum has a time period of 1 second.

Statement II: It takes precisely one second to move between the two extreme positions. In the light of the above statements,

choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are False
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false
solution: Second pendulum has a time period of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> so statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> is false but from one extreme to other it takes only half the time period so statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> is true.

subject: Physics
q: The resistance of a wire is $R$. It is bent at the middle by $180^{\circ}$ and both the ends are twisted together to make a shorter wire. The resistance of the new wire is
question: The resistance of a wire is $R$. It is bent at the middle by $180^{\circ}$ and both the ends are twisted together to make a shorter wire. The resistance of the new wire is
solution:
Resistance of wire $(R)=\rho \frac{l}{A}$ If wire is bent in the middle then
$$
\begin{aligned}
& l^{\prime}=\frac{l}{2}, A^{\prime}=2 \mathrm{~A} \\
& \therefore \text { New resistance, } \mathrm{R}^{\prime}=\rho \frac{l^{\prime}}{\mathrm{A}^{\prime}} \\
& =\frac{\rho \frac{l}{2}}{2 A}=\frac{\rho l}{4 A}=\frac{R}{4} \text {. } \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: If microwaves, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>‐</mo></math>rays, infrared, gamma rays, ultraviolet, radio waves and visible parts of the electromagnetic spectrum are denoted respectively by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> the following is the arrangement in ascending order of the wavelength
question: If microwaves, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi><mo>‐</mo></math>rays, infrared, gamma rays, ultraviolet, radio waves and visible parts of the electromagnetic spectrum are denoted respectively by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>U</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> the following is the arrangement in ascending order of the wavelength
solution: The electromagnetic (EM) spectrum is the range of all types of EM radiation. The γ‐ray has a maximum frequency or minimum wavelength; since the wavelength is inversely proportional to frequency, while radio wave has a minimum frequency and maximum wavelength. The order of wavelength will be; Gamma rays G < X-rays X < Ultra violet U < Visible rays V < Infrared rays I < Microwaves  M < Radio waves R.

q: Equivalent resistance of the following network is ________ Ω.
question: Equivalent resistance of the following network is ________ Ω.
solution:
In the above diagram, 6Ω is short circuit. Therefore, we can remove it.

In the left part, 2 Ω and 2 Ω are in parallel combination, which will give us 1 Ω which again in turn is in series combination with the 2 Ω(adjacent to 3 Ω resistor), therefore net contribution of this part will be 1 Ω+2 Ω=3 Ω. 

Now, all three 3 Ω are in parallel across point A & B, therefore Req=3×13=1 Ω

subject: Physics
q: To know the resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math> of a galvanometer by half deflection method, a battery of emf <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow></msub></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is used to deflect the galvanometer by angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math> . If a shunt of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is needed to get half deflection the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> are related by the equation:
question: To know the resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math> of a galvanometer by half deflection method, a battery of emf <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></mrow></msub></math> and resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is used to deflect the galvanometer by angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">θ</mi></math> . If a shunt of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is needed to get half deflection the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>G</mi></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> are related by the equation:
solution:
Let resistance S is connected with galvanometer of resistance G, than

IG=VR+G


Now, as shown below figure a shunt S is connected in parallel with the galvanometer for half deflection in it, that is IG' = IG2, than

 I=VR+GSG+S


As, shunt and galvanometer are in parallel  then VG=VS
IG'G=I-IG'S

⇒IG'G+S=IS
⇒IG2=ISG+S     ∵IG'=IG2

On, substituting the values of IG and I in above equation

V2R+G=VR+GSG+S×SG+S
⇒12R+G=SRG+S+GS
⇒RG+S+GS=2SR+G
⇒RG+RS+GS=2SR+G
⇒RG=2SR+G-SR+G
⇒RG=SR+G

subject: Physics
q: An object is taken to a height above the surface of earth at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> from the centre of the earth. Where radius of earth, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The percentage decrease in the weight of the object will be
question: An object is taken to a height above the surface of earth at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> from the centre of the earth. Where radius of earth, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6400</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math>. The percentage decrease in the weight of the object will be
solution:
Acceleration due to gravity at height h is given by, g'=g1+hR2

For distance from the centre r=54R height h=R4,

⇒g'=g1+142=1625g

Change in weight 

∆W=mg-mg'⇒∆W=mg1-1625

⇒∆W=925mg

Percentage change =925×100%

=36%

subject: Physics
q: A series LCR circuit is subjected to an ac signal of $200 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$. If the voltage across the inductor $(\mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{mH})$ is $31.4 \mathrm{~V}$, then the current in this circuit is _____ .
question: A series LCR circuit is subjected to an ac signal of $200 \mathrm{~V}, 50 \mathrm{~Hz}$. If the voltage across the inductor $(\mathrm{L}=10 \mathrm{mH})$ is $31.4 \mathrm{~V}$, then the current in this circuit is _____ .
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Voltage across inductor } \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{IX}_{\mathrm{L}} \\ & 31.4=\mathrm{I}[\mathrm{L} \omega] \\ & 31.4=\mathrm{I}[\mathrm{L}(2 \pi \mathrm{f})] \\ & 31.4=\mathrm{I}\left[10 \times 10^{-3}(2 \times 3.14) \times 50\right. \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}=10 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A body of mass 100 g is moving in circular path of radius 2 m on vertical plane as shown in figure. The velocity of the body at point $A$ is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The ratio of its kinetic energies at point $B$ and $C$ is :
(Take acceleration due to gravity as $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
question:
A body of mass 100 g is moving in circular path of radius 2 m on vertical plane as shown in figure. The velocity of the body at point $A$ is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The ratio of its kinetic energies at point $B$ and $C$ is :
(Take acceleration due to gravity as $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \times 100+0=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mV}_{\mathrm{B}}^2+\mathrm{mg}\left(\mathrm{R}-\frac{\mathrm{R} \sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\ & 100=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}^2+2 \mathrm{gR}\left(1-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right] \\ & \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}^2=100-20(2-\sqrt{3}) \\ & \left.\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}^2=60+20 \sqrt{3}\right) \\ & \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mV}_{\mathrm{B}}^2=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{2}(60+20 \sqrt{3}) \\ & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}(100)=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mV} \\ & 2 \\ & 100=\mathrm{mg}\left(\frac{3 \mathrm{R}}{2}\right) \\ & 100 \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{C}}^2=60 \\ & \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{C}}^2=40 \\ & \mathrm{~K} . \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mV} \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}^2=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}(40) \\ & \mathrm{K} . \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{60+20 \sqrt{3}}{40}=\frac{3}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=\frac{3+\sqrt{3}}{2}\end{aligned}$

Another body B of mass <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> collides with A with a velocity which is half <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> the instantaneous velocity <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> of A. question: A body A of mass <math><mi>m</mi></math> is moving in a circular orbit of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> about a planet. Another body B of mass <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> collides with A with a velocity which is half <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> the instantaneous velocity <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> of A. The collision is completely inelastic. Then, the combined body:
options: continues to move in a circular orbit
Escapes from the Planet's Gravitational field
Falls vertically downwards towards the planet
starts moving in an elliptical orbit around the planet
solution:
Conserving momentum
m2v2+mv=m+m2vf
vf=2mV4×3m2=5V6
vf<vorb=v thus the combined mass will go on to an elliptical path

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere of radius R carries a charge Q+q distributed uniformly over its volume. A very small point like piece of it of mass m gets detached from the bottom of the sphere and falls down vertically under gravity. This piece carries charge q. If it acquires a speed ν when it has fallen through a vertical height y (see figure), then (assume the remaining portion to be spherical)
question: A solid sphere of radius R carries a charge Q+q distributed uniformly over its volume. A very small point like piece of it of mass m gets detached from the bottom of the sphere and falls down vertically under gravity. This piece carries charge q. If it acquires a speed ν when it has fallen through a vertical height y (see figure), then (assume the remaining portion to be spherical)
solution:
By using total energy conservation
ΔKE+(ΔPE)electo +(ΔPE)gravitation =0

12mV2+kQqR+y-kQqR+(-mgy)=0

12mV2=mgy+kQq1R-1R+y;  V2=2gy+2kQqmyR(R+y)

V2=2yqQ4πε0R(R+y)m+g

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass (5±0.5) kg is moving with a velocity of (20±0.4) m s-1. Its kinetic energy will be
question: A body of mass (5±0.5) kg is moving with a velocity of (20±0.4) m s-1. Its kinetic energy will be
solution:
The kinetic energy is given by,

K=12mv2

Differentiating the above equation,

dK=12(v2dm+2mvdv)

Dividing by K,

⇒dKK=dmm+2dvv

⇒dK1000=0.55+2×0.420

⇒dK=100+40=140

⇒KE=(1000±140)J

subject: Physics
q: A thin convex lens and a thin concave lens are kept in contact and are co-axial. Which of the following statements is correct for this combination of two lenses ?
question: A thin convex lens and a thin concave lens are kept in contact and are co-axial. Which of the following statements is correct for this combination of two lenses ?
options: Focal length of the lens system will change if the positions of two lenses are interchanged
solution:
The equivalent focal length $F$ of two thin lenses in contact is given by $\dfrac{1}{F} = \dfrac{1}{f_1} + \dfrac{1}{f_2}$

For a convex lens, the focal length is positive, so $f_1 = |f_{\text{convex}}|$

For a concave lens, the focal length is negative, so $f_2 = -|f_{\text{concave}}|$

Substituting these values, we get $\dfrac{1}{F} = \dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{convex}}|} - \dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{concave}}|}$

If $|f_{\text{convex}}| > |f_{\text{concave}}|$, then $\dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{convex}}|} < \dfrac{1}{|f_{\text{concave}}|}$

This implies $\dfrac{1}{F} < 0$, so $F$ is negative.

A negative equivalent focal length indicates that the combination behaves as a diverging or concave lens.

Interchanging the positions of the lenses does not change the equivalent focal length since $\dfrac{1}{f_1} + \dfrac{1}{f_2} = \dfrac{1}{f_2} + \dfrac{1}{f_1}$.

Answer: behaves as concave lens if $|f_{\text{convex}}| > |f_{\text{concave}}|$

question: The difference of temperature in a material can convert heat energy into electrical energy. To harvest the heat energy, the material should have

subject: Physics
q: Two monochromatic light beams have intensities in the ratio 1:9. An interference pattern is obtained by these beams. The ratio of the intensities of maximum to minimum is
question: Two monochromatic light beams have intensities in the ratio 1:9. An interference pattern is obtained by these beams. The ratio of the intensities of maximum to minimum is
solution: $\frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{\left(\sqrt{I_1}+\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2}{\left(\sqrt{I_1}-\sqrt{I_2}\right)^2} \Rightarrow \frac{(4)^2}{(2)^2} \Rightarrow \frac{16}{4}=4$

subject: Physics
q: Three identical spheres of mass m , are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of length a. When released, they interact only through gravitational force and collide after a time $\mathrm{T}=4$ seconds. If the sides of the triangle are increased to length 2 a and also the masses of the spheres are made 2 m , then they will collide after _____ seconds.
question: Three identical spheres of mass m , are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle of length a. When released, they interact only through gravitational force and collide after a time $\mathrm{T}=4$ seconds. If the sides of the triangle are increased to length 2 a and also the masses of the spheres are made 2 m , then they will collide after _____ seconds.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{m}^{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{G}^{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{a}^{\mathrm{z}} \\ & \mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{x}}\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]^y[\mathrm{~L}]^{\mathrm{z}} \\ & \mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{L}^{3 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}} \mathrm{T}^{-2 \mathrm{y}} \\ & \mathrm{x}-\mathrm{y}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{y} \\ & -2 \mathrm{y}=1 \Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=-\frac{1}{2}, \mathrm{x}=-\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow 3 \mathrm{y}+\mathrm{z}=0 \\ & \mathrm{z}=-3 \mathrm{y}=\frac{3}{2}\end{aligned}$
Hence
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T} \propto \mathrm{~m}^{-1 / 2} \mathrm{G}^{-1 / 2} \mathrm{a}^{3 / 2} \\ & \mathrm{~T} \propto\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}^3}{\mathrm{~m}}\right)^{1 / 2} \\ & \mathrm{~T}=4 \times\left(\frac{2^3}{2}\right)^{1 / 2}=8 \mathrm{~s}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The refractive index of the material of a concave lens is $\mu$. It is immersed in a medium of refractive index $\mu_1$. A parallel beam of light is incident on the lens. The path of the emergent rays when $\mu_1>\mu$ is:
question: The refractive index of the material of a concave lens is $\mu$. It is immersed in a medium of refractive index $\mu_1$. A parallel beam of light is incident on the lens. The path of the emergent rays when $\mu_1>\mu$ is:
solution:
If a lens of refractive index $\mu$ is immersed in a medium of refractive index $\mu_1$, then its focal length in medium is given by
$$
\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}}=\left({ }_{\mathrm{m}} \mu_l-1\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}\right)-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)
$$
If $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}$ is the focal length of lens in air, then
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}}=\left({ }_{\mathrm{a}} \mu_l-1\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)\right. \\
&\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}}=\frac{\left({ }_{\mathrm{a}} \mu_l-1\right)}{\left({ }_{\mathrm{m}} \mu_l-1\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$
If $\mu_1>\mu$, then $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}$ and $\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{a}}$ have opposite signs and the nature of lens changes i.e. a convex lens diverges the light rays and concave lens converges the light rays. Thus given option (a) is correct.
